lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market commercial printers using offset, flexographic, gravure, letterpress, engraving, and digital processes to repeatedly serve business, institutional, agency, retail, packaging-label, direct-mail, and trade customers on a job-order or program basis.
lens.excluded_activities: Screen printing, book printing, publishing, captive in-house print rooms, pure brokers without transferable operations, hobby shops, non-control financing vehicles, and owner-dependent shops without recurring external customer relationships.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Job-order and program revenue priced per project, run, impression, piece, or contracted schedule, often combining prepress, printing, variable data, finishing, mailing, fulfillment, and account service.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation counts for the broader NAICS 323 printing and support subsector, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in 323111. In May 2023, printing workers were 36.61% of subsector employment, including press operators at 23.98%, binding/finishing workers at 8.58%, and prepress workers at 4.05%; graphic designers were 4.34%, while management, business/financial, computer, sales, and office roles form a sizable additional layer. I assign high exposure to estimating, order intake, content and artwork preparation, preflight, variable-data setup, customer service, planning, and administration, but low exposure to press setup, materials, finishing, maintenance, and physical quality. Inclusion of support activities and book/screen printing in NAICS 323 can bias the mix either way for 323111.
a.rationale: Commercial printing has more document-centric and creative workflow than many manufacturers: specifications, quotes, files, artwork, proofs, imposition, production tickets, schedules, and customer communication are amenable to assisted automation. Press operation, color control, substrate handling, bindery, packing, maintenance, and final physical inspection remain equipment- and operator-dependent.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-time study across 20 representative 323111 plants, split by offset, flexographic, digital, direct-mail, and label work and documenting current workflow automation, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: BLS data cover all NAICS 323, not the six-digit industry.
Occupation shares do not directly measure task exposure or existing automation.

rho.rationale: Digital job tickets, PDF-based workflows, web-to-print systems, and structured production data make quoting, preflight, proofing, scheduling, and service automation feasible within five years. Fragmented legacy MIS, custom exceptions, color and brand risk, variable customer files, cybersecurity, and small-firm change capacity prevent full implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Measured deployments from commercial printers showing sustained labor-hour savings, quote and proof cycle times, preflight escape rates, makeready, rework, user adoption, and customer acceptance would materially update the range.
caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed implementation probability.
Digital-native printers may implement faster than legacy offset-heavy plants.

e.rationale: The industry is fundamentally job-order work for external customers, so recurring accounts, equipment, staff, and workflows are usually transferable. Captive shops, microbusinesses, distressed capacity, customer books inseparable from the owner, and firms outside the true EBITDA band reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level review of the 2,058 dataset-estimated candidates covering ownership, recurring revenue, customer concentration, equipment, normalized EBITDA, and owner dependence would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The supplied n uses size-class receipts and a paper-industry margin bridge rather than observed printer EBITDA.
Large variation in print process, end market, and value-added services affects transferability.

s5.rationale: The large, fragmented mature firm base should produce retirement sales and strategic consolidation, but declining commodity segments, owner-linked customer relationships, equipment obligations, internal family succession, and closures without a going-concern transfer reduce the qualifying rate.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year denominator-matched transaction and closure database from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, and printing-industry deal advisers, paired with owner-age and succession-plan data, would materially change this probability.
caveats: No comprehensive six-digit control-transfer series was found.
Asset auctions, tuck-in customer-book purchases without operating control, and internal reorganizations must be excluded.

q.rationale: Project and run pricing can retain savings between bids, particularly for short-run, variable-data, regulated, label, fulfillment, or fast-turn work where service matters. Competitive quotes, print brokers, transparent online pricing, annual programs, and customer procurement share away recurring cost improvements.
q.change_evidence: Contract and bid samples, quote-to-win histories, price-realization data, customer concentration, renewal cadence, open-book clauses, and margin bridges following automation would establish actual capture.
caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefits.
Commodity offset work and differentiated data/fulfillment programs have very different pass-through.

d5.bridge: USPS mail volume is a downstream proxy for only part of commercial print demand. USPS reports total mail falling from 154.3 billion pieces in 2016 to 108.7 billion in 2025, First-Class Mail from 61.2 billion to 42.0 billion, and Marketing Mail from 80.9 billion to 56.8 billion. I extrapolate a slower five-year decline for the full 323111 basket because labels, packaging-related print, signage-like large-format graphics, cards, and specialized collateral are not represented by mail. The mail proxy therefore likely overstates decline for diversified printers but may understate digital substitution in forms and office collateral.
d5.rationale: Electronic statements, advertising, workflows, and customer communications continue to displace forms, transactional mail, and general collateral. Physical labels, packaging print, direct mail, event materials, premium pieces, regulated documents, and local rapid-turn work provide a durable floor.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted Census shipments and physical impressions by product class, plus printer MIS volumes segmented into mail, labels, packaging, forms, collateral, and wide-format work, would materially change the range.
caveats: USPS volumes omit non-mailed print and do not map one-for-one to printer output.
BLS occupational decline can reflect productivity and consolidation as well as demand.

o.rationale: Remaining demand is for physical printed output and usually still requires accountable file preparation, color and substrate control, production, finishing, and delivery. Some simple work can shift to customer-operated office devices, online self-service platforms, or in-house digital equipment, but complex, high-volume, variable, finished, and quality-sensitive work retains an external operator.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and lost-order reason codes quantifying in-sourcing, online self-service, local device substitution, software-only elimination, and offshore procurement by product category would materially change this value.
caveats: Software elimination belongs in d5; o addresses the supplier model for remaining quantity.
Digital presses can lower efficient scale and enable some customer in-sourcing.

narrative.executive_view: Commercial printing has an unusually document-rich manufacturing workflow and a large fragmented firm population, creating credible AI opportunity in quoting, prepress, job planning, and customer administration. The physical production core remains operator-dependent, while long-run digital substitution pressures much of the demand basket.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can parse specifications, generate and compare estimates, check files and versions, assist preflight and imposition, summarize proof changes, schedule jobs, predict exceptions, draft production tickets, and handle routine account communication. Press setup, color management, substrates, finishing, maintenance, packing, and accountable physical quality remain human and machine work.
narrative.value_capture: Fast-turn, short-run, variable-data, label, fulfillment, and specification-sensitive work can retain workflow gains through service and switching costs. Commodity jobs are readily rebid, and procurement teams, brokers, and online alternatives will pass a substantial share into price over five years.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied dataset estimates 2,058 firms in the economic band, consistent with a broad fragmented market, but actual eligibility requires verifying EBITDA, recurring accounts, equipment condition, customer concentration, and owner dependence. Retirement and consolidation support transfers, though closures and asset sales are not qualifying events.
narrative.demand_durability: Mail and transactional print volumes show sustained decline as communication digitizes. Labels, packaging-related print, direct mail, regulated pieces, premium physical media, and fast local work soften but do not remove the pressure; remaining complex work generally still needs a commercial operator.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key uncertainties are six-digit task mix, current workflow automation, customer and product mix, the split between declining mail/forms and resilient labels or packaging, actual LMM earnings, and whether owner-held relationships transfer. The supplied compensation and firm-count inputs also mix vintages and use a margin bridge.
narrative.principal_driver: Specification-heavy digital files and repetitive quote-to-proof-to-production workflows expose a meaningful share of skilled prepress, estimating, planning, and service labor.
narrative.principal_weakness: Electronic substitution shrinks major print categories, while competitive bidding and online price transparency erode long-run retention of savings.

sources.title: Printing and Related Support Activities - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: BLS reports 378,410 workers in NAICS 323 in May 2023; printing workers were 138,520 (36.61%), including 90,740 press operators (23.98%), 32,450 binding/finishing workers (8.58%), and 15,330 prepress workers (4.05%). Graphic designers were 16,420 (4.34%).

sources.title: Printing and Related Support Activities: NAICS 323
sources.supports: BLS describes the subsector as printing labels, business cards, stationery, business forms, and other materials and reports 341,000 total employees and 237,200 production and nonsupervisory employees in May 2026.

sources.title: NAICS 323111: Commercial Printing (except Screen and Books)
sources.supports: Reproduces the NAICS definition: lithographic, gravure, flexographic, letterpress, engraving, and digital printing without publishing, including job-order printing on purchased stock and quick printers.

sources.title: A Decade of Facts and Figures
sources.supports: USPS reports total mail volume declining from 154.3 billion pieces in 2016 to 108.7 billion in 2025, First-Class Mail from 61.2 billion to 42.0 billion, and Marketing Mail from 80.9 billion to 56.8 billion.

sources.title: Occupational Projections and Worker Characteristics
sources.supports: BLS projects printing press operator employment to decline from 150,200 in 2024 to 138,000 in 2034, an 8.1% decline, while still averaging 13,700 annual openings.
